1. REFERENCES 
 
The following references are applicable to this report: 
 
(1) Exemption QC-004-2018 dated 18 June, 2018  – Exemption from subsections 
605.04(1) and 605.36(1), and from paragraphs 602.60(1)(e), 605.18(h) and 
702.43(a) of the Canadian Aviation Regulations. 
(2) CAR Part VI - General Operating and Flight Rules (as at 2018-09-20) 
(3) CAR Part VII - Commercial Air Services (as at 2018-09-20) 
(4) NAVAIR 01-40AVM-1 and NAVAIR 01-40AVM-1.1T Tactical Manual  
(5) A4ALL-2-07-1-1400-AFMS  
(6) Discovery Air Defe nse Services (DADS): A4ALL -2-7-1-1400-RPT05 Safety 
Assessment.  
 
2. INTRODUCTION 
2.1 PURPOSE 
 
The purpose of this report is twofold: 
 
• To demonstrate that that the subject aircraft meet the equipment requirements to 
be eligible for a Special Certificate of Airworthiness – Limited as provided via the 
conditions of reference (1), and; 
 
• To demonstrate that the subject aircraft  complies with th e equipment 
requirements of CAR Part s VI and VII applicable to operation of the airplane for 
commercial air services under CAR Part VII, Subpart 2 — Aerial Work. 
2.2 SCOPE 
 
This report is limited to the equipment and installations only as required by the 
documents / regulations defined via section 2.1 above. 
 
2.3 APPLICABILITY 
 
This report is applicable to Mcdonnell Douglas A4N serial number 14337-14542: 
• as defined via NAVAIR 01-40AVM-1 at the time of import into Canada, and; 
• as modified in accordance with A4N C of A Application Data Package Section 4 
Modifications and Major Repairs.

3. DISCUSSION 
3.1 FACTORS RELEVANT TO THIS COMPLIANCE ANALYSIS 
 
The following assumptions are applicable to this report: 
 
(a) The Mcdonnell Douglas A4N is a single place seat configuration which requires a crew 
of 1 in the front seating position per reference.   
 
(b) The Mcdonnell Douglas A4N is a single-engine aircraft. 
 
(c) The aircraft is not a Transport Category aircraft. 
 
(d) The aircraft is not designed, configured or equipped for the commercial transport 
of passengers or Cargo. 
 
(e) The aircraft is not offered for common carriage or for hire. 
 
(f) The MTOW of the A4N is 11,136 kg. 
 
These assumptions are used to simplify the compliance analysis presented in Annex A 
to this report since there are many requirements that are not applicable to the Mcdonnell 
Douglas A4N. 
 
3.2 REQUIREMENTS COVERED BY THE CERTIFICATION BASIS 
 
The following sections address and document the proposed certification basis that will be used 
to access the subject design change. 
 
3.2.1 AIRCRAFT CERTIFICATION BASIS 
 
Make: DOUGLAS AIRCRAFT COMPANY 
  
Model: A-4N 
  
Original certification basis: Non-Type Certified 
Aircraft 
 
TCDS: N/A 
 
The Douglas A-4 Skyhawk is a single seat 
subsonic carrier-capable attack aircraft developed 
for the United States Navy and United States 
Marine Corps in the early 1950s. The first 
prototype first flew on 22 June 1954. Deliveries to Navy and 
Marine Corps squadrons commenced in late 1956 as the A-4D.

title: PDF Page 7
content:
Doc: AIR-1695-1 
Rev:  A 
Page:  3 
Date:  11-May-2018
 
 
 
THE DISCLOSURE AND PROPRIETARY NOTICES ON PAGE i APPLY TO THE DATA CONTAINED ON EACH PAGE OF THIS DOCUMENT. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
The Skyhawk remained in production until 1979, with 2,960 aircraft built. Some of the nearly 
3,000 produced remain in service with several air arms around the world. The improved A-4N 
Skyhawk for Israel entered service in early 1973, based on the A-4M models used by the U.S. 
Marine Corps. 
 
The A-4N was originally designed to a military specification. The aircraft was originally operated 
in a military role and was never civilian type certified.  Civil certification to European or Federal 
Aviation Administration standards of airworthiness was never considered as the Douglas A-4 Jet 
design remained in a military role only. 
 
Subsequent selection of the aircraft for use by Top Aces to provide operational support for 
various national and international defense commitments resulted in acceptance of the aircraft 
per the TCCA procedures specified for the registration of ex-military flight vehicles (Basis for 
Eligibility for Registration: CAR Standard 507.03 (5)(b) - Ex-military Aircraft).  This process did 
not clearly address the identification of a formal Basis of Certification for support of future design 
changes. 
 
The Top Aces fleet includes 7 Douglas A-4-N Skyhawk bearing selected serial numbers 
between 14337 and 14542, and bearing date of manufacture between 1973 and 1976.  The 
Douglas A-4N has a MTOW of 11,136 kg (24,500 lbs), which is only slightly in excess of the 
19,000 lb determinant for Part 23 commuter category.  It should also be recognized that the 
MTOW determinants were not promulgated until amendment 23-34 dated 02/17/1987. 
 
The aircraft is a light, single engine, turbofan, single pilot, high performance aircraft, and is not a 
large Transport Category aircraft.  It does not require dual controls, instruments and a co-pilot, 
and is not involved in the transport of passengers or cargo, which seems to be better described 
as a Part 23, Class III aircraft.  Also, its configuration, equipment and uses are more similar to 
high performance, aerobatic category aircraft than to a Transport Category aircraft. 
 
The following facts are considered in the determination of the proposed certification basis, and 
the subsequent demonstration of compliance. 
 
• The A-4N is operated by a single pilot from a single confined cockpit and does 
not always include civil airworthiness levels of redundancy such as dual 
controls and systems.  Conversely, it maintains unique capabilities that can 
assist in safely resolving airborne emergencies such as the drag chute or 
arresting hook. 
• The A-4N is employed by Top Aces in a military support role where it is 
permissible to operate the aircraft in a fashion that is consistent with its 
original design but which can negate the utility of some civil airworthiness 
system requirements.   In order to optimize safety from a human performance 
standpoint, it can be necessary to embody design changes with minor 
deviation.  For example: the ability to temporarily suppress TCAS and TAWS 
audio alerts to prevent spurious and distracting alarms during formation flight, 
air combat or air to ground attack simulation.

• Environmentally, the aircraft will also have unique capabilities not present on 
civil aircraft such as the ability to sublimate icing accretions via aerodynamic 
heating, and; 
• The operation of the basic aircraft was defined by NAVAIR 01-40AVM-1 and 
NAVAIR 01-40AVM-1.1T Tactical Manual.  These documents provide 
guidance, validated through the US Navy’s decades of experience with the 
role and the platform, on the safe operation and recovery of the aircraft.  
These include emergency procedures governing situations such as 
malfunction of pitot/static systems, or instrument failure.  In the event of 
complete airspeed indication failure, for instance, the A-4N can safely be 
recovered using the independent AOA indicating system alone. 
 
In recognition of the above facts, Transport Canada has agreed that the following basic 
airworthiness standard is to be applied to all modifications and repairs on the aircraft, based 
on the regulations which were in force at the time of manufacture of the first A-4N variant (per 
the TCCA meeting minutes of Reference 3): 
 
a) First production of the Douglas A-4 Skyhawk (N variant):  1973 
 
b) Basis of Certification (Acrobatic Category):  
 
Federal Aviation Administration Airworthiness Standard for Normal, utility, 
acrobatic, and commuter category airplanes, 
 
14CFR Part 23, Amendments 23-1 through 23-14. 
  
(Note that Amdt. 23-14 became effective on 20 December 1973)

4. CONCLUSION 
 
Based on the analysis presented in this report, it is concluded that Mcdonnell Douglas 
A4N serial number(s) 14337- 14542 comply with the requirements applicable to 
operation of the airplane for commercial operation under CAR 702 except as follows: 
 
(a) CAR 602.60(1)(e) – Hand-held fire extinguisher in the cockpit – TOP ACES Inc. 
has been exempted from this requirement for this aircraft fleet. See TCCA 
Document QC-004-2018. 
 
(b) CAR 602.61 & 602.63(6)(c) – Survival equipment other than that contained in the 
survival kit is not installed (considered to be operating equipment that is installed 
by the operator if required by the route being flown). 
 
(c) CAR 605.04 – A paper AFM is not carried on board the aircraft.  The iPad mini 
has been approved as an EFB via TCCA Approval of Company Operations 
Manual Rev 12 addressing the use of the iPad Mini as an 'EFB on A4N & 
Westwind, dated 18 Sep 2013 (RDIMS # AEPM 1377926) and, TOP ACES Inc. 
has received Technical Airworthiness 'Clearance' (TAC) for the use of the iPad 
Mini to be used as an 'EFB on the A4N aircraft during military missions for the 
DND/CF via DND letter 2182D-1027-835-2-2 (DTAES 3-6) dated 03-Feb-2014.  
See also TCCA Document QC-004-2018. 
 
 
(d) CAR 605.14(h) – Oil temperature indicators – Not applicable – The A4N is not 
equipped with air-cooled piston engines.  It features a turbojet engine, however, 
the aircraft is equipped with Exhaust Gas Temperature Gauge in the cockpit. 
 
(e) CAR 605.36 – Altitude Alerting System or Device – TOP ACES Inc has been 
exempted from this CARs requirement for this aircraft fleet. See TCCA Document 
QC-004-2018. 
 
 
(f) CAR 605.38 – An ELT has been installed by Discovery Air Defense. 
 
(g) CAR 702.42(1)(b) – A means of illumination or other means to detect the 
formation of ice – The pilot uses the wing fence  on the wing leading edge to 
detect the formation of ice, which is situated at his shoulder.  At night, the pilot 
illuminates the leading edge with his flashlight. 
 
(h) CAR 702.43(a) - Auto-pilot – TOP ACES Inc. has been exempted from this CARs 
requirement for this aircraft fleet. See TCCA Document QC-004-2018. 
 
NOTE 
 
Refer to the compliance checklist for factors that mitigate the fact that the 
airplane is non-compliant with the above requirements.

602.62 - Life 
Preservers and 
Flotation Devices 
(1) No person shall conduct a take-off or a landing on water in an aircraft or operate 
an aircraft over water beyond a point where the aircraft could reach shore in the 
event of an engine failure, unless a life preserver, individual flotation device or 
personal flotation device is carried for each person on board. 
The A4N operates over water up to 200 nautical miles from shore and and 
the pilot seat cushion is a Personal Floatation Device that is connected to 
the crewmember by a lanyard.   
 (2) No person shall operate a land aeroplane, gyroplane, helicopter or airship at 
more than 50 nautical miles from shore unless a life preserver is carried for each 
person on board. 
See 602.62(1) above. 
 (3) No person shall operate a balloon at more than two nautical miles from shore 
unless a life preserver, individual flotation device or personal flotation device is 
carried for each person on board. 
Not Applicable 
 (4) For aircraft other than balloons, every life preserver, individual flotation device 
and personal flotation device referred to in this section shall be stowed in a position 
that is easily accessible to the person for whose use it is provided, when that 
person is seated. 
Complies – The pilot seat cushion is a Personal Floatation Device that is 
connected to the crewmember by a lanyard.   
602.63 - Life Rafts 
and Survival 
Equipment — Flights 
over Water 
(1) No person shall operate over water a single-engined aeroplane, or a multi-
engined aeroplane that is unable to maintain flight with any engine failed, at more 
than 100 nautical miles, or the distance that can be covered in 30 minutes of flight 
at the cruising  speed filed in the flight plan or flight itinerary, whichever distance is 
the lesser, from a suitable emergency landing site unless life rafts are carried on 
board and are sufficient in total rated capacity to accommodate all of the persons 
on board. 
  However, the pilot wears a LPSV which contains a Personal Floatation   
Device which is water activated and will Automatically Inflate the Unit. There 
is also a Life Raft in the RSSK-8A-1 Rigid Seat Survival Kit equipped with   
survival gear that exceeds the requirements of this regulation.  The RSSK-
8A-1 Rigid Seat Survival Kit is connected to the crewmember by a lanyard 
(ref.(4) page 1-74). 
 (2) Subject to subsection (3), no person shall operate over water a multi-engined 
aeroplane that is able to maintain flight with any engine failed at more than 200 
nautical miles, or the distance that can be covered in 60 minutes of flight at the 
cruising speed filed in the flight plan or flight itinerary, whichever distance is the 
lesser, from a suitable emergency landing site unless life rafts are carried on board 
and are sufficient in total rated capacity to accommodate all of the persons on 
board. 
Complies – The pilot seat cushion is a Personal Floatation Device that is 
connected to the crewmember by a lanyard.  There is also a Life Raft in 
the RSSK-8A-1 Rigid Seat Survival Kit is equipped with survival gear that 
exceeds the requirements of this regulation.  The RSSK-8A-1 Rigid Seat 
Survival Kit is connected to the crewmember by a lanyard (ref.(4) page 1-
74) and is easily accessible in case of a ditching. 
 (3) A person may operate over water a transport category aircraft that is an 
aeroplane, at up to 400 nautical miles, or the distance that can be covered in 120 
minutes of flight at the cruising speed filed in the flight plan or flight itinerary, 
whichever distance is the lesser, from a suitable emergency landing site without the 
life rafts referred to in subsection (2) being carried on board. 
Not Applicable – The A4N is not a Transport Category aircraft

(b) the air traffic control unit received a request from the person to operate the 
aircraft within that airspace before the aircraft entered the airspace; and 
(c) aviation safety is not likely to be affected. 
Noted

Alerting System or 
Device 
(1) Subject to subsection (2), no person shall conduct a take-off in a turbo-jet-
powered aeroplane unless it is equipped with an altitude alerting system or device 
that conforms to the Aircraft Equipment and Maintenance Standards. 
TOP ACES Inc. has been exempted from this CARs requirement for this 
aircraft fleet. See TCCA Document QC-004-2018.

Part VII - Commercial Air Services  
Subpart 0 - General  
700.05 – Aircraft 
Requirements 
(1) Subject to subsection (3), no Canadian air operator shall operate an aircraft in a 
commercial air service unless 
(a) a certificate of airworthiness that meets the requirements of Article 31 of the 
Convention has been issued for the aircraft; and 
(b) in the case of an aircraft registered in another contracting state, the Minister has 
authorized its operation under Part II and, where a Canadian type certificate has not 
been issued for the aircraft type, the aircraft has been approved for operation under 
Part V. 
(2) No air operator that is a citizen, permanent resident or corporation of a foreign 
state shall operate an aircraft in a commercial air service in Canada unless 
(a) the aircraft is registered in Canada under Part II or in the foreign state; and 
(b) where a Canadian type certificate has not been issued for the aircraft type, the 
aircraft has been approved for operation under Part V. 
(3) The Minister may authorize the operation of an aircraft in aerial work under 
Subpart 2 if 
(a) a special certificate of airworthiness in the restricted or limited classification has 
been issued in respect of the aircraft; or 
(b) a foreign flight authority that is the equivalent of a special certificate of 
airworthiness in the restricted or limited classification has been issued in respect of 
the aircraft and validated by the Minister under section 507.05. 
Noted – This document is provided in support of the application for a

702.42 - Night and 
IMC Flights 
(1) No person shall operate an aircraft at night unless the aircraft is equipped with  
 (a) at least one landing light; and Complies – See 605.16. 
 
 (b) if the aircraft is operated in icing conditions, a means of illumination or other 
means to detect the formation of ice. 
Complies – The pilot inspects the fence on the wing leading edge to detect 
the formation of ice, which is situated at his shoulder.  At night, the pilot 
illuminates the leading edge with his flashlight.. (ref. pages 7-1 and 7-2). 
 (2) No person shall operate a multi-engined aircraft in IMC unless the aircraft is 
equipped with 
 
 (a) two generators or two alternators, each of which is driven by a separate engine 
or by a rotor drive train; and 
Not applicable – The A4N is a single engine aircraft. 
 
 (b) two independent sources of energy, at least one of which is not a battery, and 
each of which is able to drive all flight instruments requiring a source of energy and 
is installed so that the failure of one instrument or one source of energy will affect 
neither the energy supply to the remaining instruments nor the other source of 
energy. 
Complies – See 605.16(1)(d).  Also, failure of any source of energy will not 
affect the energy supply to the remaining instruments nor the other source 
of energy.

702.43 - Additional 
Equipment for Single-
pilot Operations 
No air operator shall operate an aircraft on a single-pilot operation in IFR flight 
unless the aircraft is equipped with 
(a) an auto-pilot that is capable of operating the aircraft controls to maintain flight 
and manoeuvre the aircraft about the lateral and longitudinal axes; 
(b) a headset with a boom microphone or equivalent and a transmit button on the 
control column; and 
(c) a chart holder that is equipped with a light and that is placed in an easily 
readable position. 
TOP ACES Inc. has been exempted from this CARs requirement for this 
aircraft fleet. See TCCA Document QC-004-2018. 
702.44 - Shoulder 
Harnesses 
No air operator shall operate an aircraft unless the pilot seat and any seat beside 
the pilot seat are equipped with a safety belt that includes a shoulder harness. 
Complies – See 605.22. 
 
702.45 - External 
Load Equipment 
No air operator shall operate an aircraft carrying an external load unless the 
attachment device is authorized in a supplemental type certificate or in an 
airworthiness approval relating to the operational configuration of the aircraft. 
Complies – Each external load is specifically authorized via TA TR 343, 
Airplane Flight Manual Supplement, Rev 6.
